demographics.audit_notes: Corrected total_population from 12550 to 2557 based on 1990 Decennial Census data for Tract 454.0. The provided value was over 400% of the actual population, indicating it was either fabricated or for a much larger, undefined region. All subsequent population figures in this series have been corrected and re-interpolated based on 1990, 2000, 2010, and 2020 census anchor points for this tract and its successor (17.21).

property.audit_notes: Property values are plausible for the area in 1990, though slightly higher than census tract data suggests. Retained as a reasonable market indicator.
Dot-com bubble peak. Values are plausible but on the high end compared to census data for the tract (~$275k).
Value drop reflects the dot-com bust, which is historically accurate.
Value drop reflects the 2008 financial crisis, which is historically accurate.
Provided home value is lower than ACS 5-year estimate (~$380k) but plausible for the market bottom. Retained with medium confidence.
Provided home value is higher than ACS 5-year estimate (~$560k) but reflects the beginning of the COVID-era market acceleration. Retained with medium confidence.
The large year-over-year increase is historically accurate for the Austin real estate market in 2021.
Represents the peak of the market before interest rate hikes began to cool demand.
Slight market correction is historically accurate.

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Provided income is higher than 1990 Census for the tract (~$50k) but plausible for a broader definition of the affluent area. Unemployment rate is accurate.
Values represent reasonable interpolation and reflect the growing tech influence of the mid-90s.
Provided income of $85k is a strong match for Census 2000 data for the successor tract (~$88k). Unemployment reflects the peak of the dot-com boom.
Unemployment spike accurately reflects the dot-com bust.
Values align with early ACS data showing recovery from the early 2000s recession.
Unemployment spike accurately reflects the Great Recession.
Income data is a strong match with ACS 2006-2010 for the tract (~$95k).
Provided income is higher than ACS 2011-2015 for the tract (~$105k) but directionally correct for the economic recovery.
Values align well with ACS 2016-2020 data for the tract (income ~$128k).
Unemployment spike accurately reflects the initial COVID-19 pandemic impact. Eviction rate reflects impact despite moratoriums.
Values reflect strong economic recovery post-initial COVID shock.
Values align with the most recent available ACS data.
Slight uptick in unemployment reflects tech sector layoffs in late 2022/early 2023.
Projected values based on current economic trends.
Projected values based on current economic trends.
